Supercut · creative playbook

protein powder

What the longest-running paid social video ads in this market keep doing — counted, with the footage to prove it.

2026-07-12 · 55 ads studied across 40 brands

How we picked these ads

Advertisers kill losing ads fast, so an ad still delivering after a month has earned a look. We searched 216 ads in this market — including its heaviest spenders, so the pool reflects where real budgets go, not just what got views — and kept the ones running 30+ days and seen in the last 30. An AI watched each surviving ad and answered a fixed set of questions about it; code then counted the answers. 0 ads that turned out to sell something else were rejected — they are listed at the bottom. No single ad is called a winner here. The findings are patterns that repeat across 55 ads from 40 different brands.

What the studied ads sell

protein powder13 of 55
whey protein7 of 55
amino acid supplements4 of 55
plant-based protein3 of 55
clear protein powder3 of 55
whey protein isolate3 of 55
22 one-off segments22 of 55

Top takeaways

45 of 55Show the product within the first 3 seconds to match high-performing patterns.
41 of 55Hire creators to film content as they represent the dominant look in the market.
53 of 55Ensure every video works without sound by using clear text or visuals.
  1. Use energetic creators to solve common protein problems ugc problem-solution · 21 ads
  2. Let creators ask questions directly to the viewer creator testimonial · 14 ads
  3. Lead with strong visual hooks and fast cuts product demo · 7 ads
  4. Flash energetic clips for a secondary brand feel lifestyle montage · 5 ads
  5. Lead with bold claims in static images still image with text · 4 ads
  6. Use the founder to call out specific problems founder on camera · 4 ads
  7. What to test next
  8. What to double-check

Chapter 1 · ugc problem-solution

Use energetic creators to solve common protein problems

21 ads 19 brands 190 median days live 4 median cuts in first 10s

This is the most common format in the pool. It relies on high energy and clear product demonstrations to hold attention.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

bold claim7 of 21
visual hook5 of 21
social proof4 of 21
question2 of 21
curiosity tease1 of 21
offer or discount1 of 21
problem callout1 of 21

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds16 of 21
3 to 10 seconds5 of 21

How they ask for the click

soft10 of 21
hard9 of 21
none2 of 21

How to shoot it

  • Keep the energy level high throughout the clip. (18 of 21 ads)
  • Show the product within the first 3 seconds. (16 of 21 ads)
  • Record a full demonstration of how to use the powder. (17 of 21 ads)
  • Add captions so the ad works with the sound off. (20 of 21 ads)

Examples that lasted

Serena Neel
Opens with: “So a lot of people are confused why I bought a hundred orange juices.” · 518 days live · 10.0M plays · watch the ad
hauskris
Opens with: “A lot of people say that their protein shake recipe tastes like a milkshake” · 400 days live · 2.7M plays · watch the ad
1 Up Nutrition
Opens with: “4 Reasons Why I Love this Lemon Italian Ice Clear Protein” · 215 days live · 12.6M plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Only 1 ad in this group focuses on a problem callout hook despite the format name.

Chapter 2 · creator testimonial

Let creators ask questions directly to the viewer

14 ads 12 brands 184 median days live 1 median cuts in first 10s

These ads feel like personal recommendations. They usually have very few cuts and a medium pace.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

social proof4 of 14
question4 of 14
bold claim3 of 14
visual hook2 of 14
problem callout1 of 14

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds11 of 14
3 to 10 seconds3 of 14

How they ask for the click

none6 of 14
soft5 of 14
hard3 of 14

How to shoot it

  • Use questions or social proof to start the video. (8 of 14 ads)
  • Keep the footage looking like it was shot by a creator. (13 of 14 ads)
  • Maintain a medium pacing with few jump cuts. (13 of 14 ads)
  • Include a full demonstration of the product mixing. (10 of 14 ads)

Examples that lasted

Fit&Lean Nutrition
Opens with: “As a mom who's LOST 100 POUNDS” · 316 days live · 181k plays · watch the ad
Brooke Lynn
Opens with: “The Clean Simple Eats simply vanilla protein powder is my favorite because it's so versatile.” · 254 days live · 779k plays · watch the ad
Katie’s life
Opens with: “what protein powder do you use please katie?” · 200 days live · 3.1M plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Almost no one in this category uses a problem callout hook style.

Chapter 3 · product demo

Lead with strong visual hooks and fast cuts

7 ads 5 brands 205 median days live 9 median cuts in first 10s

Demo ads rely on showing the powder or liquid in motion. They have the second highest number of cuts per 10 seconds.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

visual hook4 of 7
social proof2 of 7
question1 of 7

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds6 of 7
later1 of 7

How they ask for the click

none4 of 7
soft2 of 7
hard1 of 7

How to shoot it

  • Start with a striking visual to grab attention. (4 of 7 ads)
  • Ensure the product appears in the first 3 seconds. (6 of 7 ads)
  • Keep the tone energetic and the pacing fast. (5 of 7 ads)
  • Design the entire ad to work perfectly on mute. (7 of 7 ads)

Examples that lasted

albert_cancook
Opens with: “Protein Chocolate Crack for your Valentine” · 219 days live · 1.6M plays · watch the ad
Clean Simple Eats
Opens with: “PROTEIN POWDER TOASTED MARSHMALLOW” · 205 days live · 3.8M plays · watch the ad
MuscleSport
Opens with: “FAT METABOLIZING” · 232 days live · 49k plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Hard calls to action are rare here, with most ads using no CTA at all.

Chapter 4 · lifestyle montage

Flash energetic clips for a secondary brand feel

5 ads 5 brands 180 median days live 10 median cuts in first 10s

These use the highest number of cuts to show a variety of scenes. They are fast and energetic.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

visual hook2 of 5
bold claim2 of 5
curiosity tease1 of 5

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds4 of 5
3 to 10 seconds1 of 5

How they ask for the click

hard3 of 5
soft2 of 5

How to shoot it

  • Use a fast pacing with 10 cuts in the first 10 seconds. (4 of 5 ads)
  • Ensure the tone is highly energetic across all clips. (5 of 5 ads)
  • Use a hard call to action at the end. (3 of 5 ads)
  • Feature the product early in the montage. (4 of 5 ads)

Examples that lasted

albert_cancook
227 days live · 7.2M plays · watch the ad
Yovana Mendoza
Opens with: “What I eat in a day 35 yo mom of 2” · 212 days live · 142k plays · watch the ad
PerfectAmino by BodyHealth
Opens with: “BUILD LEAN MUSCLE” · 180 days live · 2.6M plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Nearly all creators in this group only show a glimpse of the product rather than a full demo.

Chapter 5 · still image with text

Lead with bold claims in static images

4 ads 3 brands 183.5 median days live 0 median cuts in first 10s
Only 4 ads fit this format — read this chapter as a hint, not a rule.

This is a brand-heavy format. It uses zero cuts and depends entirely on the written word.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

bold claim3 of 4
social proof1 of 4

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds4 of 4

How they ask for the click

hard2 of 4
soft2 of 4

How to shoot it

  • Make a bold claim as your primary hook style. (3 of 4 ads)
  • Ensure the product is visible immediately. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Stick to a professional brand feel rather than a creator look. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Maintain a neutral or aspirational emotional tone. (3 of 4 ads)

Examples that lasted

Frog Fuel Actual
Opens with: “YOUR GREENS POWDER IS LAZY. OURS GETS TO WORK.” · 227 days live · 2k plays · watch the ad
Transparent Labs
Opens with: “14 Tasty Flavors” · 173 days live · 21k plays · watch the ad
Clean Simple Eats
Opens with: “CREATIVE FLAVORS THAT NAIL IT EVERY TIME!” · 194 days live · 0 plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Zero ads in this format include a product demonstration.

Chapter 6 · founder on camera

Use the founder to call out specific problems

4 ads 3 brands 204 median days live 4 median cuts in first 10s
Only 4 ads fit this format — read this chapter as a hint, not a rule.

Founders bridge the gap between brand and creator. They are effective at highlighting why the product exists.

The first three seconds, side by side

How these ads open

problem callout2 of 4
bold claim2 of 4

When the product first appears

first 3 seconds4 of 4

How they ask for the click

soft2 of 4
hard1 of 4
none1 of 4

How to shoot it

  • Start the video with a specific problem or bold claim. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Display the product in the first 3 seconds. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Use a soft call to action to maintain trust. (2 of 4 ads)
  • Keep the tone energetic and active. (3 of 4 ads)

Examples that lasted

Becoming the Ultimate Human with Gary Brecka
Opens with: “If you're making your coffee and you want to add essential amino acids” · 217 days live · 9.2M plays · watch the ad
PerfectAmino by BodyHealth
Opens with: “PERFECT AMINO BUILDS 6X THE PROTEIN OF WHEY” · 191 days live · 8.2M plays · watch the ad
Wellah Nutrition
Opens with: “COACHES, YOU KNOW THAT PROTEIN IS EVERYTHING” · 31 days live · 208k plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Founders almost never use a social proof hook style in this pool.

What to test next

Problem-Hook Test for Creators

Hypothesis
If we use a problem callout hook in a creator testimonial, we will increase interest by filling a gap in common tactics.
Why this test
Only 1 out of 14 creator testimonials currently uses a problem callout hook style.
Call it on
Hook Rate
Shot notes
  • Record a creator complaining about clumpy protein powder.
  • Use a handheld phone for a raw creator feel.
  • Ensure the product label is visible by 3 seconds.
Risk
The audience may find the problem callout too negative for a testimonial format.
Draft script
Does your protein powder always end up clumpy? I found the solution. This blends perfectly while I'm on the move. Click below to try it.

Silent Social Proof Demo

Hypothesis
If we combine a visual product demo with social proof text, we will capture muted scrollers effectively.
Why this test
4 out of 7 product demos use visual hooks, and 7 out of 7 must work without sound.
Call it on
Click Through Rate
Shot notes
  • Film a high-speed pour of the powder into water.
  • Overlay a customer quote in large text during the pour.
  • Use at least 9 cuts in 10 seconds for high energy.
Risk
The text may distract from the visual quality of the product pour.
Draft script
(No Dialogue) Text: 'The only powder that doesn't taste like chalk.' Visual: Powder hits water, blends instantly, person takes a drink and smiles. CTA: Shop Now.

Founder Social Proof Hook

Hypothesis
If the founder leads with social proof, we will combine authority with trust.
Why this test
0 out of 4 founder ads currently use a social proof hook style.
Call it on
Conversion Rate
Shot notes
  • Founder stands in a kitchen holding the product.
  • The first 3 seconds show a '5-star rating' graphic.
  • Founder maintains a sincere tone rather than high energy.
Risk
Social proof might feel less authentic coming from the brand owner.
Draft script
There is a reason why 40,000 people have switched to our protein this year. We solved the top three issues people had with whey isolate. Join the community today.

What to double-check

Everything above comes from an AI watching ads one at a time and code counting its answers. Here is where that process was noisy, and what it threw out.

Ads rejected as off-market (0)

The search also returned ads selling something else. They were dropped before any counting, so they do not touch the numbers above.

Nothing was rejected in this run.

How reliable was the watching?

We measured the editing pace of every ad twice: once by the AI's eye, once by scene-detection software. The AI was off by 2.4 cuts on average, and within 2 cuts of the software on 62% of ads. The cut counts printed in the chapters are the software's, not the AI's.

Where a spoken hook could be checked against the ad library's own transcript (23 ads), the AI's quote matched 23 times and disagreed 0 times. The other 32 ads had on-screen-text hooks, no hook, or no transcript — nothing to check against.

The AI reported firm format labels for every ad in the pool.

Assumptions this report makes

  • "Still delivering" means the ad was seen in the ad library within the last 30 days. The data offers no direct "currently live" flag.
  • Days-running and play counts come from the ad library and are taken at face value.
  • A long-running ad earned study, but nothing here proves any single ad was profitable. Only the repeated patterns are conclusions.
  • Spoken quotes were transcribed by the AI from the audio; where the ad library had its own transcript, we cross-checked (see above).

Study ranking: ads are ordered for the example lists by a score of longevity ×0.5 + engagement ×0.3 + formatConfidence ×0.2, each part normalized within this pool, with at most one ad per brand in any list. Inside the engagement part, one share counts as 100 plays — passing an ad on is a deliberate endorsement, being watched often is not.

Made with Supercut. Days-running and play counts come from the ad library data; "still delivering" means seen in the last 30 days, the closest signal the data offers to "currently live".
